title: Redcurrant jelly
subtitle: Clear, jewel-bright jelly strained from fresh redcurrants.
excerpt: Redcurrants simmered with water and strained overnight through muslin, the ruby-red juice then boiled with sugar to a firm, trembling jelly. Serves with roast lamb, as a glaze, or spread on bread. Makes about 3 jars.
sourceNotes: Redcurrant jelly. Redcurrants have been cultivated in British kitchen gardens since the 16th century, and redcurrant jelly appears throughout 19th-century British preserving manuals. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) contains the standard recipe. The jelly is a classical accompaniment to roast lamb and venison.

content.text: Redcurrant jelly is strained, not potted with the fruit. The pulp goes through muslin overnight; only the clear juice goes into the jars. This is what gives it the jewel colour and the clean, bright flavour. Do not rush the straining: squeezing the bag clouds the jelly. The dripping takes time.

items.prepNote: adjust: 750 g per 500 ml strained juice

content.text: Put {{redcurrants}} of redcurrants and {{water}} of water in a jam pan. Bring to a simmer and cook for 15 minutes, crushing the fruit occasionally with a spoon, until the berries have fully collapsed. Pour into a muslin-lined colander set over a large bowl. Leave to drip overnight without pressing the bag. Measure the strained juice: you need about 750 g of sugar per 500 ml of juice.
Boil to setting point and jar

Return the juice to the jam pan and add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar. Stir over low heat until dissolved, then bring to a rolling boil. Boil hard for 8 to 10 minutes until setting point is reached: 104.5°C on a thermometer, or a drop on a cold saucer that wrinkles when pushed. Skim any foam. Ladle into sterilised jars and seal.

items.fix: Do not press the bag. Only gravity should move the juice. A slightly cloudy jelly is still delicious but loses the visual appeal.

items.cause: Did not reach setting point, or too much water in the juice.
items.fix: Return to the boil and test every 5 minutes. Redcurrants are high in pectin so setting should be reliable if you reach 104.5°C.

items.cause: Fully ripe berries, which are lower in acid.
items.fix: Use slightly underripe redcurrants if possible. The jelly is intended to be tart and sharp.

content.text: Redcurrant jelly is a kitchen staple in British cooking: on the side of a roast leg of lamb, stirred into a game sauce, or spread on bread with butter. The short window in late July and August makes it a preserving priority. A jar made in summer and eaten at Christmas with cold lamb is one of the small satisfactions of seasonal cooking.
